stem: What information remains after the phase-encoding gradient is switched off?

options.text: A position-dependent phase offset
A permanent position-dependent T1 change
A different proton density at each location
A continuous frequency difference caused by the absent gradient

explanation: The gradient briefly changes precession frequency, causing spins at different positions to accumulate different phase. Once it is off, frequencies equalize but the phase offsets remain long enough to encode position.

stem: Why is the pulse sequence repeated with multiple phase-encoding gradient amplitudes?

options.text: Each amplitude excites a different chemical element
Each amplitude acquires a different line of k-space
Each amplitude changes the main field to a new tesla value
Each amplitude independently measures T1 without an echo

explanation: A single readout cannot resolve both in-plane dimensions. Repeating the sequence with stepped phase encoding samples different spatial frequencies and fills successive k-space lines.

objective: Relate phase matrix to scan time.
stem: What is the usual effect of increasing the number of phase-encoding steps while TR and averages are unchanged?

options.text: Scan time decreases
Scan time is unchanged because all steps occur simultaneously
Scan time increases
Only receiver bandwidth changes

explanation: Each phase-encoding step generally requires another sequence repetition, so more phase lines increase acquisition time. This differs from adding readout samples, which are collected within one echo.

stem: At a fixed phase field of view, what does increasing the phase matrix do?

options.text: It worsens phase-direction spatial resolution
It increases slice thickness
It removes all phase-direction motion artifact
It improves phase-direction spatial resolution

explanation: More phase samples divide the same field of view into smaller pixels, improving in-plane resolution. The tradeoffs include longer acquisition and lower signal per pixel.

stem: Which phase-encoding step contributes most directly to the center of k-space?

options.text: The step with zero or very small phase-encoding amplitude
The step with the strongest positive gradient only
The step with the longest receiver dwell time
The step performed after longitudinal recovery is complete

explanation: Low phase-encoding amplitudes represent low spatial frequencies and populate central k-space. Strong positive and negative steps populate progressively more peripheral lines.
